title: HTML Certification
description: Learn the building blocks of web development, from text elements to forms, tables, and page layout.

lessons.title: Introduction to HTML
lessons.explanation: Welcome to web development! HTML stands for **HyperText Markup Language**. It is a markup language used to structure and lay out content on a webpage, rather than a programming language (which executes program logic). Every standard webpage uses the `<html>` tag as the root element to wrap all other elements on the page.
lessons.examples:
A basic HTML element has an opening tag, content, and a closing tag. The `` tag acts as the root container: ```html

This is a paragraph inside the root HTML element.

```
lessons.tips: Every HTML tag starts with a `<` and ends with `>`. Closing tags also include a forward slash `/` before the tag name.
lessons.challenge: Create a paragraph (`p`) element containing the text 'Welcome to DevloperPath'.
lessons.starterCode:
<html>
  <head>
    <title>Introduction</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <!-- Write your HTML code below this line -->

  </body>
</html>

tests.description:
Your code should contain a paragraph (

) element.
tests.assertion: const p = doc.querySelector('p'); return !!p;

tests.description: Your paragraph element should contain the text 'Welcome to DevloperPath'.
tests.assertion: const p = doc.querySelector('p'); return p && p.textContent.trim().replace(/\s+/g, ' ') === 'Welcome to DevloperPath';

lessons.title: HTML Document Structure
lessons.explanation:
Every HTML document requires a specific skeleton to be understood correctly by browsers. The `<html>` element is the **root element** of the page and wraps all other elements. Inside the `<html>` root, a page is divided into two sections:
1. The `<head>` tag: Contains metadata, search settings, and links to stylesheets. This section is invisible to users viewing the page.
2. The `<body>` tag: Contains all visible elements (headings, text, images, forms, and links) that users interact with.
lessons.examples:
A complete HTML document structure looks like this:

```html
<html>
  <head>
    <!-- Document settings go here -->
  </head>
  <body>
    <!-- Visible content goes here -->
  </body>
</html>
```
lessons.tips: Browsers rely on this structure to parse and render the webpage properly. The body tag is where all the visuals live.
lessons.challenge: Create a complete HTML skeleton. Inside the provided `<html>` tags, add a `<head>` element, followed by a `<body>` element.
lessons.starterCode:
<html>
  <!-- Create a head and body element inside -->
</html>

tests.description: Your code should contain an opening and closing <html> tag.
tests.assertion: return /<html>/i.test(code) && /<\/html>/i.test(code);

tests.description: Your code should contain an opening and closing <head> tag.
tests.assertion: return /<head>/i.test(code) && /<\/head>/i.test(code);

tests.description: Your code should contain an opening and closing <body> tag.
tests.assertion: return /<body>/i.test(code) && /<\/body>/i.test(code);

tests.description: Your <head> and <body> elements must be nested inside the <html> element.
tests.assertion: const html = doc.querySelector('html'); return html && html.querySelector('head') && html.querySelector('body');

lessons.title: HTML Headings
lessons.explanation: HTML headings are defined with the `<h1>` to `<h6>` tags. `<h1>` defines the most important heading (usually the largest), while `<h6>` defines the least important heading (usually the smallest). Headings help search engines understand the structure of your content.
lessons.tips: Only use one `<h1>` per page for search engine optimization (SEO) best practices.
lessons.challenge: Create an `h1` heading element containing the text 'DevloperPath' and an `h2` heading element containing the text 'Interactive Editor'.
lessons.starterCode:
<html>
  <head>
    <title>My Headings</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <!-- Write your HTML headings below this line -->

  </body>
</html>

tests.description: You should create an <h1> element.
tests.assertion: const h1 = doc.querySelector('h1'); return !!h1;

tests.description: Your <h1> element should contain the text 'DevloperPath'.
tests.assertion: const h1 = doc.querySelector('h1'); return h1 && h1.textContent.trim() === 'DevloperPath';

tests.description: You should create an <h2> element.
tests.assertion: const h2 = doc.querySelector('h2'); return !!h2;

tests.description: Your <h2> element should contain the text 'Interactive Editor'.
tests.assertion: const h2 = doc.querySelector('h2'); return h2 && h2.textContent.trim() === 'Interactive Editor';

lessons.title: HTML Paragraphs
lessons.explanation:
The HTML `

` element defines a paragraph. Paragraphs are **block-level** elements. This means they always start on a new line and stretch out to take up the full available width of their container. Browsers also automatically add vertical margins before and after paragraphs.
lessons.examples:
```html

This is the first paragraph.

This is the second paragraph.

```
lessons.tips:
White spaces or multiple lines inside a `

` element will be collapsed by the browser into a single space.
lessons.challenge: Create two separate paragraph (`p`) elements. The first should contain 'Start small.', and the second should contain 'Dream big.'.
lessons.starterCode:
<html>
  <head>
    <title>My Paragraphs</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <!-- Write your paragraph elements below this line -->

  </body>
</html>

tests.description:
You should create two

elements.
tests.assertion: return doc.querySelectorAll('p').length === 2;

tests.description: The first paragraph should contain the text 'Start small.'.
tests.assertion: const ps = doc.querySelectorAll('p'); return ps[0] && ps[0].textContent.trim() === 'Start small.';

tests.description: The second paragraph should contain the text 'Dream big.'.
tests.assertion: const ps = doc.querySelectorAll('p'); return ps[1] && ps[1].textContent.trim() === 'Dream big.';

lessons.title: Build a Personal Portfolio Page
lessons.explanation: In this workshop, you will build a personal portfolio landing page step-by-step using headings and paragraphs.

steps.title: Main Heading
steps.challenge: Create an `h1` element containing the text 'John Doe's Portfolio'. This will be the main title of your page.
steps.starterCode:
<html>
  <head>
    <title>Portfolio</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <!-- Write your HTML code below this line -->

  </body>
</html>

tests.description: Your code should contain an <h1> element.
tests.assertion: const h1 = doc.querySelector('h1'); return !!h1;

tests.description: Your <h1> element should contain the text 'John Doe\'s Portfolio'.
tests.assertion: const h1 = doc.querySelector('h1'); return h1 && h1.textContent.trim() === "John Doe's Portfolio";

steps.title: Section Subheading
steps.challenge: Below your `h1` element, add an `h2` subheading containing the text 'About Me'.
steps.starterCode: John Doe's Portfolio

tests.description: Your code should still contain the <h1> element with 'John Doe\'s Portfolio'.
tests.assertion: const h1 = doc.querySelector('h1'); return h1 && h1.textContent.trim() === "John Doe's Portfolio";

tests.description: Your code should contain an <h2> element.
tests.assertion: const h2 = doc.querySelector('h2'); return !!h2;

tests.description: Your <h2> element should contain the text 'About Me'.
tests.assertion: const h2 = doc.querySelector('h2'); return h2 && h2.textContent.trim() === 'About Me';

steps.title: Introductory Paragraph
steps.challenge: Below your `h2` element, add a paragraph (`p`) element containing the text 'I am a web developer learning HTML on DevloperPath!'.
steps.starterCode:
John Doe's Portfolio

About Me

tests.description: Your code should contain an <h1> and <h2> element with correct texts.
tests.assertion: const h1 = doc.querySelector('h1'); const h2 = doc.querySelector('h2'); return h1 && h1.textContent.trim() === "John Doe's Portfolio" && h2 && h2.textContent.trim() === 'About Me';

tests.description:
Your code should contain a

element.
tests.assertion: const p = doc.querySelector('p'); return !!p;

tests.description:
Your

element should contain the text 'I am a web developer learning HTML on DevloperPath!'.
tests.assertion: const p = doc.querySelector('p'); return p && p.textContent.trim() === 'I am a web developer learning HTML on DevloperPath!';

lessons.title: HTML Anchor Links
lessons.explanation: HTML links are hyperlinks. You can click on a link to jump to another document or section. Links are defined with the `` (anchor) tag. The key attribute is `href`, which specifies the destination URL. The text between the opening `` and closing `` tags is called the **anchor text** or **link text**, which is what the user clicks on.
lessons.tips: Add the attribute `target="_blank"` to open the link in a new tab.
lessons.challenge: Create a link (`a`) that points to 'https://devloperpath.com' and has the anchor text 'Explore DevloperPath'.

tests.description: You should create an anchor (<a>) element.
tests.assertion: return doc.querySelector('a') !== null;

tests.description: Your anchor element should have an href attribute set to 'https://devloperpath.com'.
tests.assertion: const a = doc.querySelector('a'); return a && a.getAttribute('href') === 'https://devloperpath.com';

tests.description: Your anchor element should have text content 'Explore DevloperPath'.
tests.assertion: const a = doc.querySelector('a'); return a && a.textContent.trim() === 'Explore DevloperPath';

lessons.title: HTML Images
lessons.explanation: HTML images are defined with the `` tag. The `` tag is empty, which means it contains attributes only and does not have a closing tag. The `src` attribute specifies the URL of the image, and the `alt` attribute provides alternate text description if the image cannot be loaded.
lessons.tips: The `alt` attribute is extremely important for accessibility (screen readers) and SEO.
lessons.challenge: Create an image (`img`) element with the `src` set to 'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1542831371-29b0f74f9713' and an `alt` description of 'Code on a screen'.

tests.description: You should create an img element.
tests.assertion: return doc.querySelector('img') !== null;

tests.description: Your img element should have the correct src attribute.
tests.assertion: const img = doc.querySelector('img'); return img && img.getAttribute('src') === 'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1542831371-29b0f74f9713';

tests.description: Your img element should have an alt attribute with 'Code on a screen'.
tests.assertion: const img = doc.querySelector('img'); return img && img.getAttribute('alt') === 'Code on a screen';

lessons.title: HTML Unordered Lists
lessons.explanation: An unordered list starts with the `<ul>` tag. Each list item starts with the `<li>` tag. By default, the list items will be marked with bullets (small black circles).
lessons.tips: Unordered lists are commonly used to create navigation menus in web development.
lessons.challenge: Create an unordered list containing three items: 'HTML', 'CSS', and 'JavaScript'.
lessons.starterCode:
<html>
  <head>
    <title>Unordered Lists</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <!-- Create your unordered list below -->

  </body>
</html>

tests.description: You should create a <ul> element.
tests.assertion: return doc.querySelector('ul') !== null;

tests.description: Your <ul> element should contain exactly three <li> elements.
tests.assertion: const lis = doc.querySelectorAll('ul li'); return lis.length === 3;

tests.description: Your list items should contain the text content 'HTML', 'CSS', and 'JavaScript' in order.
tests.assertion: const lis = doc.querySelectorAll('ul li'); return lis[0] && lis[0].textContent.trim() === 'HTML' && lis[1] && lis[1].textContent.trim() === 'CSS' && lis[2] && lis[2].textContent.trim() === 'JavaScript';

lessons.title: HTML Ordered Lists
lessons.explanation: An ordered list starts with the `<ol>` tag. Each list item starts with the `<li>` tag. The list items will be marked with numbers (1, 2, 3...) instead of bullet points.
lessons.tips: Ordered lists are used when the sequence of items is important, like instructions or rankings.
lessons.challenge: Create an ordered list containing three steps: 'Learn basic syntax', 'Build projects', and 'Apply for jobs'.
lessons.starterCode:
<html>
  <head>
    <title>Ordered Lists</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <!-- Create your ordered list below -->

  </body>
</html>

tests.description: You should create an <ol> element.
tests.assertion: return doc.querySelector('ol') !== null;

tests.description: Your <ol> element should contain three <li> elements.
tests.assertion: return doc.querySelectorAll('ol li').length === 3;

tests.description: Your list items should contain the correct texts in order.
tests.assertion: const lis = doc.querySelectorAll('ol li'); return lis[0] && lis[0].textContent.trim() === 'Learn basic syntax' && lis[1] && lis[1].textContent.trim() === 'Build projects' && lis[2] && lis[2].textContent.trim() === 'Apply for jobs';

lessons.title: Build a Recipe Page
lessons.explanation: In this workshop, you will build a dynamic recipe page step-by-step using links, images, ordered and unordered lists.

steps.title: Recipe Title & Image
steps.challenge: Create an `h1` element containing 'Favorite Pizza Recipe'. Below the heading, create an `img` element with `src` set to 'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1513104890138-7c749659a591' and `alt` set to 'Delicious pizza'.
steps.starterCode:
<html>
  <head>
    <title>Recipe Page</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <!-- Write your HTML code below this line -->

  </body>
</html>

tests.description: Your code should contain an <h1> containing the text 'Favorite Pizza Recipe'.
tests.assertion: const h1 = doc.querySelector('h1'); return h1 && h1.textContent.trim() === 'Favorite Pizza Recipe';

tests.description: Your code should contain an element with the correct src.
tests.assertion: const img = doc.querySelector('img'); return img && img.getAttribute('src') === 'https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1513104890138-7c749659a591';

tests.description: Your element should have the alt description 'Delicious pizza'.
tests.assertion: const img = doc.querySelector('img'); return img && img.getAttribute('alt') === 'Delicious pizza';

steps.title: Ingredients Unordered List
steps.challenge: Below your image, add an `h2` element with the text 'Ingredients'. Under it, create an unordered list (`ul`) containing three `li` items: 'Pizza Dough', 'Tomato Sauce', and 'Mozzarella Cheese'.
steps.starterCode: Favorite Pizza Recipe

tests.description: Your code should contain an <h2> with the text 'Ingredients'.
tests.assertion: const h2 = doc.querySelector('h2'); return h2 && h2.textContent.trim() === 'Ingredients';

tests.description: Your code should contain a <ul> with three <li> elements.
tests.assertion: const lis = doc.querySelectorAll('ul li'); return lis.length === 3;

tests.description: Your list items should contain the texts 'Pizza Dough', 'Tomato Sauce', and 'Mozzarella Cheese' in order.
tests.assertion: const lis = doc.querySelectorAll('ul li'); return lis[0] && lis[0].textContent.trim() === 'Pizza Dough' && lis[1] && lis[1].textContent.trim() === 'Tomato Sauce' && lis[2] && lis[2].textContent.trim() === 'Mozzarella Cheese';

steps.title: Instructions Ordered List
steps.challenge: Below the ingredients, add an `h2` element with the text 'Instructions'. Under it, create an ordered list (`ol`) containing three `li` steps: 'Roll the dough', 'Spread the sauce', and 'Bake at 450°F'.
steps.starterCode:
Favorite Pizza Recipe

Ingredients

  • Pizza Dough
  • Tomato Sauce
  • Mozzarella Cheese

tests.description: Your code should contain an <h2> with the text 'Instructions'.
tests.assertion: const h2s = doc.querySelectorAll('h2'); return Array.from(h2s).some(h2 => h2.textContent.trim() === 'Instructions');

tests.description: Your code should contain an <ol> with three <li> elements.
tests.assertion: const lis = doc.querySelectorAll('ol li'); return lis.length === 3;

tests.description: Your ordered list items should contain the correct steps in order.
tests.assertion: const lis = doc.querySelectorAll('ol li'); return lis[0] && lis[0].textContent.trim() === 'Roll the dough' && lis[1] && lis[1].textContent.trim() === 'Spread the sauce' && lis[2] && lis[2].textContent.trim() === 'Bake at 450°F';

steps.title: Link to More Recipes
steps.challenge: At the bottom of your page, add an anchor link (`a`) with the `href` attribute set to 'https://devloperpath.com' and the anchor text 'Learn more recipes on DevloperPath!'.
steps.starterCode:
Favorite Pizza Recipe

Ingredients

  • Pizza Dough
  • Tomato Sauce
  • Mozzarella Cheese

Instructions

  1. Roll the dough
  2. Spread the sauce
  3. Bake at 450°F

tests.description: Your code should contain an anchor link (<a>) element.
tests.assertion: return doc.querySelector('a') !== null;

tests.description: Your anchor link should point to 'https://devloperpath.com'.
tests.assertion: const a = doc.querySelector('a'); return a && a.getAttribute('href') === 'https://devloperpath.com';

tests.description: Your anchor link should contain the text 'Learn more recipes on DevloperPath!'.
tests.assertion: const a = doc.querySelector('a'); return a && a.textContent.trim() === 'Learn more recipes on DevloperPath!';

lessons.title: HTML Tables
lessons.explanation: HTML tables allow web developers to arrange data into rows and columns. A table is defined with the `<table>` tag. Each table row is defined with the `<tr>` tag. A table header is defined with the `<th>` tag. A table cell/data is defined with the `<td>` tag.
lessons.tips: Use headers (`th`) at the top of your columns to make tables readable and accessible.
lessons.challenge: Create a table with a header row containing two headers: 'Feature' and 'Benefit'. Add one data row containing two table cells: 'Interactive Editor' and 'Hands-on practice'.

tests.description: You should create a <table> element.
tests.assertion: return doc.querySelector('table') !== null;

tests.description: Your table should contain exactly two rows (<tr>).
tests.assertion: return doc.querySelectorAll('table tr').length === 2;

tests.description: The first row should contain two table header cells (<th>) with text 'Feature' and 'Benefit'.
tests.assertion: const ths = doc.querySelectorAll('table tr:first-child th'); return ths.length === 2 && ths[0].textContent.trim() === 'Feature' && ths[1].textContent.trim() === 'Benefit';

tests.description: The second row should contain two table data cells (<td>) with text 'Interactive Editor' and 'Hands-on practice'.
tests.assertion: const tds = doc.querySelectorAll('table tr:nth-child(2) td'); return tds.length === 2 && tds[0].textContent.trim() === 'Interactive Editor' && tds[1].textContent.trim() === 'Hands-on practice';

lessons.title: HTML Inputs & Labels
lessons.explanation: The HTML `<input>` element is the most used form element. An input element can be displayed in many ways, depending on the `type` attribute (like `text`, `checkbox`, `radio`, `submit`, etc.). The `<label>` element defines a label for a form element. It provides usability improvement for mouse users: if the user clicks on the text within the label element, it toggles the control.
lessons.tips: Always match the label's `for` attribute with the input's `id` attribute to link them.
lessons.challenge: Create a label element containing 'Email:' and an input element of type 'text' with the `id` set to 'email-field'. Make sure to set the `for` attribute of the label to link to the input.
lessons.starterCode:
<html>
  <head>
    <title>Inputs and Labels</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <!-- Create your label and input below -->

  </body>
</html>

tests.description: You should create a <label> element.
tests.assertion: return doc.querySelector('label') !== null;

tests.description: You should create an <input> element.
tests.assertion: return doc.querySelector('input') !== null;

tests.description: Your input element should be of type 'text' and have the id 'email-field'.
tests.assertion: const input = doc.querySelector('input'); return input && input.getAttribute('type') === 'text' && input.getAttribute('id') === 'email-field';

tests.description: Your label element should have a 'for' attribute matching the input's id and contain the text 'Email:'.
tests.assertion: const label = doc.querySelector('label'); return label && label.getAttribute('for') === 'email-field' && label.textContent.trim() === 'Email:';

lessons.title: HTML Forms & Submit Buttons
lessons.explanation: An HTML form is used to collect user input and submit it to a server. The form container is defined by the `<form>` tag, which wraps all input elements and the submit button. The `<form>` tag uses the `action` attribute to specify the destination URL where the form data should be sent upon submission. To trigger this, we use a submit button configured as `<button type="submit">`.
lessons.tips: Always specify `type="submit"` for the submit button in a form, otherwise it defaults to submit but being explicit is good practice.
lessons.challenge: Create a form element with an `action` attribute of '/register'. Inside the form, add a button with `type="submit"` containing the text 'Join DevloperPath'.
lessons.starterCode:
<html>
  <head>
    <title>Forms</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <!-- Create your form and submit button below -->

  </body>
</html>

tests.description: You should create a <form> element.
tests.assertion: return doc.querySelector('form') !== null;

tests.description: Your form element should have an action attribute set to '/register'.
tests.assertion: const form = doc.querySelector('form'); return form && form.getAttribute('action') === '/register';

tests.description: Your form should contain a <button> element.
tests.assertion: const btn = doc.querySelector('form button'); return !!btn;

tests.description: Your button should have the attribute type='submit' and contain the text 'Join DevloperPath'.
tests.assertion: const btn = doc.querySelector('form button'); return btn && btn.getAttribute('type') === 'submit' && btn.textContent.trim() === 'Join DevloperPath';

lessons.title: Build a Survey Form
lessons.explanation: In this workshop, you will build a survey submission form complete with label linkages, a text input, a submit button, and a table showing past entries.

steps.title: Form Container & Text Input
steps.challenge: Create a `form` element with an `action` attribute set to '/submit-feedback'. Inside the form, create a `label` element containing 'Your Feedback:' and an `input` element of type 'text' with an `id` of 'feedback-input'. Link the label to the input using the `for` attribute.
steps.starterCode:
<html>
  <head>
    <title>Feedback Survey</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <!-- Write your HTML code below this line -->

  </body>
</html>

tests.description: Your code should contain a <form> element with action set to '/submit-feedback'.
tests.assertion: const form = doc.querySelector('form'); return form && form.getAttribute('action') === '/submit-feedback';

tests.description: Your form should contain a <label> linked to an <input> with id 'feedback-input'.
tests.assertion: const label = doc.querySelector('form label'); const input = doc.querySelector('form input'); return label && input && label.getAttribute('for') === 'feedback-input' && input.getAttribute('id') === 'feedback-input' && input.getAttribute('type') === 'text';

tests.description: Your label should contain the text 'Your Feedback:'.
tests.assertion: const label = doc.querySelector('form label'); return label && label.textContent.trim() === 'Your Feedback:';

steps.title: Form Submit Button
steps.challenge: Inside the form (below the label and input), add a button with `type="submit"` containing the text 'Send Feedback'.
steps.starterCode:
<html>
  <head>
    <title>Feedback Survey</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <form action="/submit-feedback">
      <label for="feedback-input">Your Feedback:</label>
      <input type="text" id="feedback-input">
      <!-- Add submit button below -->
    </form>
  </body>
</html>

tests.description: Your form should contain a button element.
tests.assertion: const btn = doc.querySelector('form button'); return !!btn;

tests.description: Your button should be of type 'submit'.
tests.assertion: const btn = doc.querySelector('form button'); return btn && btn.getAttribute('type') === 'submit';

tests.description: Your button should contain the text 'Send Feedback'.
tests.assertion: const btn = doc.querySelector('form button'); return btn && btn.textContent.trim() === 'Send Feedback';

steps.title: Submissions Table
steps.challenge: Below the form, add a `table` displaying previous submissions. The table should have one header row (`tr`) containing two header cells (`th`): 'User' and 'Comment'. Add one data row (`tr`) below it containing two data cells (`td`): 'Alice' and 'Great platform!'.
steps.starterCode:
<html>
  <head>
    <title>Feedback Survey</title>
  </head>
  <body>
    <form action="/submit-feedback">
      <label for="feedback-input">Your Feedback:</label>
      <input type="text" id="feedback-input">
      <button type="submit">Send Feedback</button>
    </form>
    <!-- Add table below the form -->

  </body>
</html>

tests.description: Your code should contain a <table> element.
tests.assertion: return doc.querySelector('table') !== null;

tests.description: Your table should contain exactly two rows (<tr>).
tests.assertion: return doc.querySelectorAll('table tr').length === 2;

tests.description: The first row should contain header cells with 'User' and 'Comment'.
tests.assertion: const ths = doc.querySelectorAll('table tr:first-child th'); return ths.length === 2 && ths[0].textContent.trim() === 'User' && ths[1].textContent.trim() === 'Comment';

tests.description: The second row should contain data cells with 'Alice' and 'Great platform!'.
tests.assertion: const tds = doc.querySelectorAll('table tr:nth-child(2) td'); return tds.length === 2 && tds[0].textContent.trim() === 'Alice' && tds[1].textContent.trim() === 'Great platform!';
